content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

December 17, 1990

Elizabeth G. Clark
Deputy City Attorney
City of Torrance
3031 Torrance Blvd.
Torrance, CA 90503

Re: Your Request for Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our File No. I-90-679

Dear Ms. Clark:

We have received your letter dated October 26, 1990, seeking
written confirmation of telephone advice previously provided to
you by this agency. You had sought advice concerning whether the
limitations on contributions and transfer prohibitions provisions
of the Political Reform Act ("the Act")¹ - and specifically of
"Proposition 73" - permitted a member of the Torrance City Council
to use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to another candidate's
campaign dinner. We are able only to confirm part of the advice
provided to you.

Because your query does not involve a pending governmental
decision, we are treating your request, and the confirmation provided, as informal advice.²

You were advised, and your letter indicates, that a council-
member was not restricted in using campaign funds for the purchase
of a ticket to another candidate's campaign fundraiser, provided
that candidate was running for an office other than a legislative
office.

_______________________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. All refer-
ences to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California
Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Sec-
tion 83114; Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

File No. I-90-679
Page 2

The federal court's decision in Service Employees International Union et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission, et al.
(U.S. District Court, Eastern District of California, No. CIVS-89-0433-LKK-JFK) initially invalidated the limitations on contributions as provided for by "Proposition 73," and also invalidated the Act's transfer prohibitions to the extent the prohibitions served to further the contribution limits otherwise found invalid. The judge subsequently issued a partial stay of his ruling and resumed application of Proposition 73's contribution limitations and transfer prohibition to candidates for legislative offices only.³

In view of the court's reinstatement of the transfer provisions of Proposition 73 with respect to legislative candidates, the Commission has advised that candidates may not transfer campaign funds to legislative candidates. Therefore, it would not be permitted for a councilmember to use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to a legislative candidate's fundraising event.

It is also not permitted for a councilmember to use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to another local candidate's fundraising event if that candidate was subject to a local contribution limit premised on something other than a fiscal year basis. The Commission has advised that the provisions of Proposition 73 restricting transfers of contributions between candidates remains valid when a candidate is subject to an otherwise constitutional contribution limit.

Finally, although not a component of the Proposition 73 discussion, we wish to advise you that Section 85202 imposes a "trust" on a candidate's use of contributions received, limiting such use for expenses associated with either running for office or holding the office once elected. Section 85801 requires that expenditures to seek office must be reasonably related to a political purpose, and expenditures to hold office must be reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose. This section also requires that any expenditure of campaign funds which confer a substantial personal benefit shall be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

In determining whether an expenditure of campaign funds maintained the requisite relationship to a political, legislative or governmental purpose, various factors must be examined. In your query, such questions would include whether the councilmember was

_______________
³ Following the district court's rulings, the United States Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit and the United States Supreme Court refused to grant a stay, pending appeal, of the district court's decision. An appeal of the district court's rulings has been filed with the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit.

File No. I-90-679
Page 3

(also) running for office, and for what office the other candidate
was running. Since you did not provide any information concerning
these questions, we are able to provide you with only the foregoing general advice.

I hope this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. Please contact me if you have any further questions
regarding this matter at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Jonathan S. Rothman
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JSR:ken

Enclosure

sections.question: Whether the initiation of contributions and transfer prohibition provisions of the Political Reform Act and specifically of Proposition 73 permitted a member of the Torrance City Council to use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to another candidate's campaign dinner.
sections.conclusion: A councilmember was not restricted in using campaign funds for the purchase of a ticket to another candidate's campaign fundraiser, provided that candidate was running for an office other than a legislative office. However, it is not permitted for a councilmember to use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to a legislative candidate's fundraising event, nor to a local candidate's event if that candidate was subject to a local contribution limit on a non-fiscal year basis. Additionally, Section 85202 imposes a trust on campaign fund use limiting it to expenses associated with running for or holding office, and Section 85801 requires such expenditures be reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.
sections.question_synthetic: May a city council member use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to another candidate's campaign fundraiser under Proposition 73's contribution and transfer restrictions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Campaign funds may be used for fundraiser tickets to non-legislative candidates, but not to legislative candidates or local candidates with non-fiscal-year-based contribution limits; all campaign expenditures must satisfy the trust requirements and reasonably relate to political, legislative, or governmental purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a confirmation of prior telephone advice treated as informal advice (per footnote 2). No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers, but clear question and conclusion content present. Federal court case information and statutory context integrated throughout rather than in separate sections.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether the initiation of contributions and transfer prohibition provisions of the Political Reform Act and specifically of Proposition 73 permitted a member of the Torrance City Council to use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to another candidate's campaign dinner.

CONCLUSION: A councilmember was not restricted in using campaign funds for the purchase of a ticket to another candidate's campaign fundraiser, provided that candidate was running for an office other than a legislative office. However, it is not permitted for a councilmember to use campaign funds to purchase a ticket to a legislative candidate's fundraising event, nor to a local candidate's event if that candidate was subject to a local contribution limit on a non-fiscal year basis. Additionally, Section 85202 imposes a trust on campaign fund use limiting it to expenses associated with running for or holding office, and Section 85801 requires such expenditures be reasonably related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirms telephone advice regarding whether a Torrance City Council member may use campaign funds to purchase tickets to other candidates' fundraisers, clarifying the application of Proposition 73's transfer restrictions following federal court rulings and adding statutory limitations from Sections 85202 and 85801.
